entries.title: Question checklist
entries.content: [Title] Question checklist [Content] You may find this checklist helpful when thinking about the questions you want to ask your doctor about your disease and treatment. If your doctor gives you answers that you don't understand, ask for clarification. - What type of breast cancer do I have? - Has the cancer spread? If so, how far? How fast is it growing? - What treatment do you recommend and why? - Are there other treatment choices for me? If not, why not? - What are the risks and possible side effects of each treatment? - How long will treatment take? Will I have to stay in hospital? - How much will treatment cost? - Will I have a lot of pain with the treatment? What will be done about this? - Are the latest tests and treatments for this type of cancer available in this hospital? - Are there any clinical trials or research studies I could join? - How frequently will I need check-ups after treatment? - Who should I see for my check-up appointments? - Are there any complementary therapies that might help me? - Should I change my diet during or after treatment? - If the cancer comes back, how will I know? ![](_page_69_Picture_0.jpeg)

entries.title: ablation
entries.content:
[Title] ablation
[Content] See ovarian ablation.

entries.title: adjuvant therapy
entries.content:
[Title] adjuvant therapy
[Content] A treatment given with or shortly after another treatment to enhance its effectiveness.

entries.title: advanced cancer
entries.content:
[Title] advanced cancer
[Content] Cancer that is unlikely to be cured. It may be limited to its original site (primary cancer) or may have spread to other parts of the body (secondary or metastatic cancer).

entries.title: anti-oestrogens
entries.content:
[Title] anti-oestrogens
[Content] Drugs used to treat cancers that depend on the female hormone oestrogen to grow.

entries.title: areola
entries.content:
[Title] areola
[Content] The brownish or pink rim of tissue around the nipple of the breast.

entries.title: aromatase inhibitors
entries.content:
[Title] aromatase inhibitors
[Content] Drugs that help prevent the growth of oestrogen-dependent cancer cells by reducing the amount of oestrogen in a postmenopausal woman's body.

entries.title: atypical ductal hyperplasia
entries.content:
[Title] atypical ductal hyperplasia
[Content] An abnormal but non-cancerous condition of the cells in the lining of the milk ducts in the breast.

entries.title: axilla
entries.content:
[Title] axilla
[Content] The armpit.

entries.title: axillary lymph nodes
entries.content:
[Title] axillary lymph nodes
[Content] Lymph nodes in and around the armpit. axillary surgery

The removal of some lymph nodes in the armpit to check whether the cancer has spread.

entries.title: axillary tail
entries.content:
[Title] axillary tail
[Content] Breast tissue that extends into the armpit.

entries.title: axillary web syndrome
entries.content:
[Title] axillary web syndrome
[Content] See cording.

entries.title: benign
entries.content:
[Title] benign
[Content] Not cancerous or malignant.

entries.title: biopsy
entries.content:
[Title] biopsy
[Content] The removal of a sample of tissue for examination under a microscope.

entries.title: BRCA1 and BRCA2 genes
entries.content:
[Title] BRCA1 and BRCA2 genes
[Content] Changes on these genes increase the risk of breast or ovarian cancer.

entries.title: breast conserving surgery
entries.content:
[Title] breast conserving surgery
[Content] Surgery that removes a breast lump without removing the entire breast. Also called a lumpectomy or wide local excision.

entries.title: breast form/prosthesis
entries.content:
[Title] breast form/prosthesis
[Content] An artificial breast worn inside a bra or attached to the body with adhesive to re-create the shape of a natural breast. It can be temporary (breast form) or permanent (breast prosthesis).

entries.title: breast oedema
entries.content:
[Title] breast oedema
[Content] Swelling caused by too much fluid in the breast tissue.

entries.title: breast reconstruction
entries.content:
[Title] breast reconstruction
[Content] The surgical rebuilding of a breast after a mastectomy.

entries.title: cellulitis
entries.content:
[Title] cellulitis
[Content] An infection of the skin that can occur after lymph glands have been removed. chemotherapy

The use of drugs to treat cancer by killing cancer cells or slowing their growth.

entries.title: clear margin
entries.content:
[Title] clear margin
[Content] When the surrounding tissue removed during surgery for cancer does not contain any cancer cells.

entries.title: cold cap
entries.content:
[Title] cold cap
[Content] A cap that is connected to a cooling system and worn on the head to help prevent hair loss.

entries.title: cording
entries.content:
[Title] cording
[Content] Tight cords of tissue running down the inner arm. Cording can occur weeks or months after breast or axillary surgery. Also known as axillary web syndrome.

entries.title: ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS)
entries.content:
[Title] ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS)
[Content] Abnormal cells in the breast ducts that may increase the risk of developing invasive breast cancer.
[Keywords] DCIS

entries.title: ducts
entries.content:
[Title] ducts
[Content] The canals within the breast that pass milk from the lobules to the nipple.

entries.title: genes
entries.content:
[Title] genes
[Content] The microscopic units that determine how the body's cells grow and behave. Genes are found in every cell of the body and are inherited from both parents.

entries.title: genomic assay
entries.content:
[Title] genomic assay
[Content] A test that provides information about the risk of the cancer coming back and whether chemotherapy will be of benefit. Also called a molecular assay. grade

A score that describes how quickly a tumour is likely to grow.

entries.title: HER2
entries.content:
[Title] HER2
[Content] Human epidermal growth factor receptor 2. A type of protein found on most cells in the human body.

entries.title: hormone receptors
entries.content:
[Title] hormone receptors
[Content] Proteins in a cell that bind to specific hormones.

entries.title: hormones
entries.content:
[Title] hormones
[Content] Chemicals in the body that send information between cells to bring about changes in the body.

entries.title: hormone therapy
entries.content:
[Title] hormone therapy
[Content] A treatment that blocks the body's natural hormones, which sometimes help cancer cells grow. It is used when the cancer is growing in response to hormones. Also called endocrine therapy or hormone-blocking therapy.

entries.title: inflammatory breast cancer
entries.content:
[Title] inflammatory breast cancer
[Content] Cancer that develops in the lymphatic vessels in the skin of the breast. Rather than forming a lump, it causes the breast to become red and swollen.

entries.title: invasive breast cancer
entries.content:
[Title] invasive breast cancer
[Content] Cancer that has spread from the lining of the breast ducts or lobules into the surrounding breast tissue.

entries.title: invasive ductal carcinoma
entries.content:
[Title] invasive ductal carcinoma
[Content] Cancer that started in the milk ducts but has spread into the tissue around them. invasive lobular carcinoma

Cancer that began in the milk lobules but has spread into the breast tissue around them.

entries.title: Klinefelter syndrome
entries.content:
[Title] Klinefelter syndrome
[Content] A genetic disorder where a man has three sex chromosomes (XXY) instead of the normal two (XY).
[Keywords] XXY, XY

entries.title: lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS)
entries.content:
[Title] lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS)
[Content] Abnormal cells in the breast lobules. lobules

The milk-producing glands in the breast.
[Keywords] LCIS

entries.title: locally advanced breast cancer
entries.content:
[Title] locally advanced breast cancer
[Content] Cancer that has spread outside the breast, e.g. to the chest or lymph nodes in the armpit.

entries.title: lumpectomy
entries.content:
[Title] lumpectomy
[Content] See breast conserving surgery.

entries.title: lymphatic system
entries.content:
[Title] lymphatic system
[Content] A network of tissues, capillaries, vessels, ducts and nodes that removes excess fluid from tissues, absorbs fatty acids, transports fat, and produces immune cells.

entries.title: lymph nodes
entries.content:
[Title] lymph nodes
[Content] Small, bean-shaped structures that form part of the lymphatic system. Also called lymph glands.

entries.title: lymphoedema
entries.content:
[Title] lymphoedema
[Content] Swelling caused by a build-up of lymph fluid.

entries.title: lymph vessels
entries.content:
[Title] lymph vessels
[Content] Thin tubes that carry the body's tissue fluid (lymph) all over the body.

entries.title: malignant
entries.content:
[Title] malignant
[Content] Cancer.

entries.title: mammogram
entries.content:
[Title] mammogram
[Content] An x-ray of the breast to detect cancer. mastectomy

Surgical removal of the whole breast.

entries.title: menopause
entries.content:
[Title] menopause
[Content] When a woman stops having periods (menstruating).

entries.title: metastasis
entries.content:
[Title] metastasis
[Content] A cancer that has spread from a primary cancer in another part of the body.

entries.title: non-invasive breast cancer
entries.content:
[Title] non-invasive breast cancer
[Content] Cancer that is confined to the ducts or lobules of the breast.

entries.title: oestrogen
entries.content:
[Title] oestrogen
[Content] The primary female sex hormone that helps regulate the reproductive cycle. Oestrogen is also present in men.

entries.title: oncoplastic breast conserving surgery
entries.content:
[Title] oncoplastic breast conserving surgery
[Content] An operation in which the cancer is removed and plastic surgery techniques are used to preserve the appearance of the breast as much as possible.

entries.title: osteoporosis
entries.content:
[Title] osteoporosis
[Content] Thinning and weakening of the bones that can lead to bone pain and fractures.

entries.title: ovarian ablation
entries.content:
[Title] ovarian ablation
[Content] Stopping the ovaries from producing oestrogen by surgically removing the ovaries (oophorectomy) or giving a dose of radiotherapy to the ovaries.

entries.title: Paget's disease of the nipple
entries.content:
[Title] Paget's disease of the nipple
[Content] Cancer cells develop in the breast ducts and spread to the skin of the nipple and the areola.

entries.title: pathologist
entries.content:
[Title] pathologist
[Content] A specialist doctor who interprets the results of tests.

entries.title: pathology report
entries.content:
[Title] pathology report
[Content] A document that provides information about the cancerous tissue, including its size and location, its hormonal status, how far it has spread, how fast it is growing, and surgical margins.

entries.title: plastic surgeon
entries.content:
[Title] plastic surgeon
[Content] A medical practitioner who has had advanced surgical training in the restoration of skin and tissue to near-normal appearance and function. Also known as reconstructive surgeon.

entries.title: primary cancer
entries.content:
[Title] primary cancer
[Content] The original cancer. Cells from the primary cancer may break away and be carried to other parts of the body, where secondary cancers may form.

entries.title: progesterone
entries.content:
[Title] progesterone
[Content] A hormone produced by the ovaries that prepares the lining of the uterus (endometrium) for pregnancy. Progesterone is also present in men.

entries.title: radiotherapy
entries.content:
[Title] radiotherapy
[Content] The use of radiation, such as x-rays, gamma rays, electron beams or protons, to kill cancer cells or injure them so they cannot grow and multiply. Also called radiation therapy.

entries.title: recurrent cancer
entries.content:
[Title] recurrent cancer
[Content] Cancer that has returned after treatment of the primary cancer.

entries.title: sentinel node
entries.content:
[Title] sentinel node
[Content] The first lymph node that breast cancer cells may spread to outside the breast.

entries.title: seroma
entries.content:
[Title] seroma
[Content] A collection of fluid under a wound after surgery.

entries.title: staging
entries.content:
[Title] staging
[Content] Performing tests to determine how far a cancer has spread.

entries.title: targeted therapy
entries.content:
[Title] targeted therapy
[Content] Treatment that attacks specific particles (molecules) within cells that allow cancer to grow and spread.

entries.title: tissue expander
entries.content:
[Title] tissue expander
[Content] A balloon-like bag placed under the skin and muscle of the chest and filled with saline to create a temporary breast implant.

entries.title: tumour
entries.content:
[Title] tumour
[Content] A new or abnormal growth of tissue on or in the body. A tumour may be benign (not cancer) or malignant (cancer).

entries.title: ultrasound
entries.content:
[Title] ultrasound
[Content] A non-invasive scan that uses soundwaves to create a picture of part of the body.

entries.title: Can't find a word here?
entries.content:

entries.title: How you can help
entries.content: [Title] How you can help [Content] At Cancer Council, we're dedicated to improving cancer control. As well as funding millions of dollars in cancer research every year, we advocate for the highest quality care for cancer patients and their families. We create cancer-smart communities by educating people about cancer, its prevention and early detection. We offer a range of practical and support services for people and families affected by cancer. All these programs would not be possible without community support, great and small. Join a Cancer Council event: Join one of our community fundraising events such as Daffodil Day, Australia's Biggest Morning Tea, Relay For Life, Girls' Night In and Pink Ribbon Day, or hold your own fundraiser or become a volunteer. Make a donation: Any gift, large or small, makes a meaningful contribution to our work in supporting people with cancer and their families now and in the future. Buy Cancer Council sun protection products: Every purchase helps you prevent cancer and contribute financially to our goals. Help us speak out for a cancer-smart community: We are a leading advocate for cancer prevention and improved patient services. You can help us speak out on important cancer issues and help us improve cancer awareness by living and promoting a cancer-smart lifestyle. Join a research study: Cancer Council funds and carries out research investigating the causes, management, outcomes and impacts of different cancers. You may be able to join a study. To find out more about how you, your family and friends can help, please call your local Cancer Council. ![](_page_74_Figure_0.jpeg) Being diagnosed with cancer can be overwhelming. At Cancer Council, we understand it isn't just about the treatment or prognosis. Having cancer affects the way you live, work and think. It can also affect our most important relationships. When disruption and change happen in our lives, talking to someone who understands can make a big difference. Cancer Council has been providing infor
